title: Introduction to OOP
subtitle: Model the real world with objects that have data and behaviour
learning_objectives: Explain the difference between a class and an object
Understand what attributes (data) and methods (behaviour) are
Recognise real-world examples that map to classes and objects

sections.heading: What is Object-Oriented Programming?
sections.content:
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a way of organising code by grouping together related data and functions. Instead of having lots of loose variables and functions, you bundle them into objects.

Think of a real-world dog. A dog has:
  • Attributes (data): name, breed, age, weight
  • Behaviours (actions): bark, eat, run, sit

In Python, a class is the blueprint, and an object is one specific instance made from that blueprint. You can have many dogs, all made from the same Dog blueprint.
sections.code_example:
# Procedural approach (old way)
dog_name = "Rex"
dog_breed = "Labrador"

def dog_bark(name):
    print(name, "says: Woof!")

dog_bark(dog_name)

# OOP approach (organised)
class Dog:
    pass  # we will fill this in next lesson

# Create two dogs from the same blueprint
rex = Dog()
bella = Dog()

sections.heading: Classes, Objects, Attributes, and Methods
sections.content:
Here are the four key OOP terms:

Class — the blueprint or template (e.g. Dog, Car, BankAccount)
Object — one specific thing made from the class (e.g. my dog, your car)
Attribute — data stored on an object (e.g. dog's name, car's colour)
Method — a function that belongs to the class (e.g. dog.bark(), car.start())

OOP is used in almost every large Python program because it keeps related code together and makes it much easier to manage.
sections.code_example:
# Real-world mappings:
# Class: BankAccount
# Objects: my_account, your_account
# Attributes: owner, balance
# Methods: deposit(), withdraw(), get_balance()

# Class: Car
# Objects: my_tesla, dads_honda
# Attributes: make, model, colour, speed
# Methods: start(), accelerate(), brake()

print("OOP groups data and behaviour together!")
sections.code_output: OOP groups data and behaviour together!

exercises.title: Identify Attributes and Methods
exercises.prompt: Print the attributes and methods of a hypothetical Student class. Print 3 attributes on one line separated by commas, then 3 methods on the next line.
exercises.starter_code:
# Think about a Student class
# What data (attributes) would it have?
# What can it do (methods)?

# Print 3 attributes a Student class might have (comma-separated on one line)
# Print 3 methods a Student class might have (comma-separated on one line)
exercises.solution:
print("Attributes: name, grade, student_id")
print("Methods: study, take_exam, get_grade")
hints: Attributes are things a student HAS: name, grade, etc.
Methods are things a student DOES: study(), submit_assignment()
Just run the starter code as-is — it is already correct!

exercises.title: Create a Simple Class
exercises.prompt: Define a class called Car (just use 'pass' for the body). Create two Car objects called car1 and car2. Print the type of car1 using type().
exercises.starter_code:
# Define a Car class


# Create two Car objects


# Print the type of car1
exercises.solution:
class Car:
    pass

car1 = Car()
car2 = Car()

print(type(car1))
hints: class Car: followed by pass on the next indented line

tests.message: Output shows the Car class type

quiz.question: In OOP, what is the difference between a class and an object?
options: A class is the blueprint; an object is one specific instance made from it
A class holds data; an object holds functions
An object is bigger than a class
quiz.explanation: A class is like a cookie cutter — the blueprint. An object is like a cookie — a specific thing made using that blueprint. You can make many objects from one class.

quiz.question: What are the two kinds of things a class bundles together?
quiz.explanation: A class bundles together attributes (the data an object holds) and methods (the functions an object can perform). For example, a Dog class might have a 'name' attribute and a bark() method.

completion_message: Great start! You understand the core idea of OOP — now let us build real classes!
